title: Ackee and saltfish
subtitle: Jamaica's national dish
excerpt: Flaked salt cod folded with tinned ackee, onions, tomato, and scotch bonnet. The ackee provides a soft, buttery body that carries the salt from the fish; the scotch bonnet and thyme run through the whole dish. Cooked quickly in a hot pan once the fish is soaked and the ackee is drained. The definitive Jamaican breakfast.
sourceNotes: Ackee and saltfish became Jamaica's national dish through the convergence of the ackee fruit (brought from West Africa in the 18th century) and preserved salt cod (a colonial trade staple). The combination appears in Jamaican cooking literature from the 19th century onward and is documented as the national dish in the Jamaican Coat of Arms motto.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The saltfish must be soaked 24 to 48 hours in cold water before cooking. Change the water 3 to 4 times.

content.text: Salt cod must be properly desalinated before use. Place it in a large bowl, cover with cold water, and refrigerate. Change the water every 8 to 12 hours for 24 to 48 hours — the longer the soak, the less salty the finished dish. Taste a small piece before cooking: it should be pleasantly savoury, not aggressively salty. If still very salty, continue soaking.

Tinned ackee is already cooked and soft. Drain it gently and handle with care. The arils collapse easily and you want distinct pieces in the finished dish, not a mash. Add the ackee at the end of cooking and fold it in gently rather than stirring vigorously.
After soaking the {{salt-cod}}, place it in a pan of fresh water, bring to a boil, and simmer for 15 minutes. Drain, cool slightly, then flake the flesh away from the skin and bones. Set aside.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide frying pan over medium-high heat. Add the {{onion}}, {{pepper-red}}, and {{scotch-bonnet}} and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{tomato}} and {{thyme}} and cook for 3 more minutes. Add the flaked saltfish and {{spring-onion}} and stir to combine. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes. Add the drained {{ackee-tinned}} and fold in gently. Season with {{black-pepper}} (the dish will not need salt). Serve immediately with hard-dough bread, fried plantain, or boiled green banana.

items.fix: Extend the soak to 48 hours with 4 water changes. Always taste a piece of the boiled fish before adding it
Add the ackee last and fold it in very gently. It only needs to warm through — 1 to 2 minutes off the heat is enough

items.cause: Too little oil or too much cooking time
items.fix: The tomatoes should release enough liquid. Cook quickly over high heat — this is not a slow-cooked dish

content.text: Ackee and saltfish is the official national dish of Jamaica. It appears on the breakfast table rather than at dinner, typically served on a Saturday or Sunday alongside boiled green bananas, fried plantain, callaloo, and hard-dough bread. The combination represents the historical convergence of ingredients in Jamaican cooking: the ackee brought from West Africa, the salt cod preserved for the Atlantic trade, the scotch bonnet from Central American plant heritage. It is the dish that defines Jamaican breakfast culture for Jamaicans in Jamaica and in the diaspora.
